text: The surgeon identifies a subtle sigmoid lesion characterized by a kinking in the bowel, indicative of invasive bowel wall endometriosis. Using two instruments, the surgeon palpates a hard nodule within the anterior wall of the bowel, preparing for disc excision with double layer suture repair.

The surgeon attempts to advance an EEA sizer underneath a high sigmoid lesion but encounters a stricture preventing passage. A suture is placed through the lesion and lifted by the third arm. Using a monopolar cutting electrode at fifty to sixty watts, the surgeon excises the dense nodule in the anterior wall of the sigmoid colon. Electrocautery is applied to the bowel during the excision.

The surgeon observes the defect in the anterior sigmoid after lesion removal, preparing to suture the bowel. Three O silk sutures are placed at the three and nine o'clock positions of the incision to orient the bowel transversely. The suturing technique begins with a two-layer closure, starting by passing through the mucosa at three o'clock and exiting outward next to this point.

The surgeon closes the mucosa of the bowel incision using a running layer of 3-0 Vicryl suture, ensuring the suture enters and exits the mucosa in a box-like fashion to prevent constriction. As the suture is tightened, the incision gradually closes transversely. The needle placement shifts from side-to-side to vertical as the closure progresses. The first layer of closure is completed, followed by a second layer of interrupted O-silk imbricating sutures that incorporate bowel serosa and muscularis, covering the previous closure. The final sutures result in a normal-appearing bowel with healthy serosa and a wide open lumen. A proctoscopy and air leak test confirm no leaks.
